label: delphi-r2-p7
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 70,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "More likely than not a court invalidates at least a substantial part of the surcharge \u2014 most plausibly the increment attributable to \"conservation-incentive pricing\" above the documented 1.6x cost differential, and/or the revenue-use structure insofar as roughly 55% of proceeds fund rebates flowing predominantly to non-payers \u2014 because the stated facts contain the district's own admissions that map closely onto what Capistrano condemned. A cautious public-agency lawyer would flag this rate as high litigation risk pre-adoption; nothing in article X, section 2 or Water Code section 375 legally excuses the failure to tie the incremental rate and the rebate cross-subsidy to cost of service, so the caution here tracks genuine legal exposure rather than mere risk-aversion. The district's fallback \"not a property-related fee\" argument is very likely foreclosed by Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vejil, and the Proposition 26 \"voter-approval tax\" theory is a viable but secondary plaintiff argument if the 218 cost-of-service defense fails outright.",
  "rationales": [
    "Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 held tiered water rates must correspond to the actual marginal cost of service at that tier and rejected conservation-based incentive pricing as a substitute for cost allocation, and the district's own concession that $0.85 was derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation is close to a facial admission of that exact defect for the non-cost-justified increment.",
    "The district's concession that the rebate program (55% of restricted-fund revenue) 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' independently implicates article XIII D, section 6(b)(2)-(3) \u2014 fee revenue may not exceed, and must correlate to, the proportional cost of service attributable to the fee-paying parcels \u2014 and this cross-subsidization defect is analytically distinct from, and arguably more damaging than, the tier-pricing methodology issue.",
    "Article X, section 2's conservation mandate and Water Code section 375 are general policy provisions that California courts have treated as unable to override the specific, later-enacted, self-executing cost-of-service mandate of article XIII D, section 6(b) (this is essentially the holding rejected-argument posture in Capistrano itself), so the district's 'conservation mandate reconciliation' defense is doctrinally weak standing alone.",
    "Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vejil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 held that volumetric water charges for ongoing service are property-related fees subject to article XIII D, which makes the district's alternative 'not a property-related fee at all' argument a low-probability escape hatch.",
    "The 1.6x documented marginal-cost differential (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) gives the district a genuine, non-trivial cost-of-service anchor, so a court could plausibly sever and uphold a cost-justified portion of the surcharge up to roughly that ratio while striking only the incremental 'incentive' overlay and/or the rebate-funded 55% tranche, meaning full facial invalidation of the entire surcharge is less likely than a substantial partial invalidation."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "Cal. Const. art. XIII D, \u00a7 6(b)(1)-(5)",
    "Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493",
    "Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vejil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205",
    "Cal. Const. art. X, \u00a7 2",
    "Water Code \u00a7 375",
    "Cal. Const. art. XIII C, \u00a7 1(e) (Prop 26 'tax' definition and fee exception)",
    "Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 (cited for comparison on fee-for-service correlation; verify pinpoint applicability)",
    "Coziahr v. Otay Water Dist. (2024) \u2014 post-Capistrano tiered-rate case; verify holding and whether it narrows or reinforces Capistrano before relying on it"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a reviewing court would treat the \"conservation-incentive pricing\" and rebate cross-subsidy defects as severable from the genuinely cost-justified 1.6x portion of the rate (yielding a narrow, partial remedy/refund) versus treating the whole tiered surcharge as an indivisible, tainted rate structure requiring wholesale invalidation \u2014 this severability question, plus uncertainty about how recent post-Capistrano appellate authority (e.g., Coziahr v. Otay Water Dist., which I cannot verify with confidence) has refined the standard for conservation-linked tiered pricing, is the single largest driver of variance in my estimate."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 70.0, mean 71.4, std dev 2.8, range 70.0–78.0, middle 50% of panel between 70.0 and 72.0.

*(For themes below, "higher-probability panelists" refers to the two panelists at 72 and 78; "lower-probability panelists" refers to the five at 70.)*

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — The "in part conservation-incentive pricing" admission tracks the Capistrano defect (7 of 7).**
Every panelist reasoned that the district's own rate-study language — deriving $0.85 "in part" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — maps onto the defect condemned in *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano*. Characterizations of severity varied within the theme (from "direct doctrinal vulnerability" to "close to a per se violation for the excess increment").

**Theme B — Rebate cross-subsidy as an independent revenue-use/proportionality problem (7 of 7).**
All panelists cited the concession that 55% of proceeds fund rebates mostly benefiting non-payers, invoking some combination of §6(b)(1), (b)(2), and (b)(3). Several framed this as analytically distinct from — and potentially independently dispositive of — the pricing-methodology issue.

**Theme C — The 1.6x documented cost differential as a genuine cost anchor favoring partial rather than total invalidation (7 of 7).**
Every panelist treated the peaking-capacity/drought-reserve cost study as a real cost-of-service basis that makes severance, capping, remand, or partial upholding more likely than wholesale invalidation, and distinguished this record from the agency record in *Capistrano*.

**Theme D — Agency burden of proof under §6(b)(5) (4 of 7).**
Four panelists (three at 70; the panelist at 78) reasoned that the agency's burden of proving compliance, combined with its record concessions, shifts practical litigation risk to the district. One of these grounded the burden/preponderance standard in *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir*; the others cited the constitutional text directly.

**Theme E — Bighorn forecloses the "not a property-related fee" fallback (5 of 7).**
Five panelists (all at 70) reasoned the district's threshold argument fails under *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil*, forcing the case to be decided on cost-of-service merits. The two higher-probability panelists did not include this in their rationales (one cited *Bighorn* only in authorities; one cited it for a different proposition — see disagreements).

**Theme F — Judicial deference / no mathematical precision required (3 of 7, skewing higher-probability).**
Both higher-probability panelists (72, 78) plus one panelist at 70 invoked deference authority (*Wilde*, *Griffith*, *Coziahr*) as a countervailing consideration that could salvage the full rate or the district's blended methodology.

**Theme G — 15% conservation-education staffing as a general public benefit problem (3 of 7).**
Three panelists separately flagged the "water ambassador" allocation as funding a general governmental/public-education service rather than service to the paying parcels.

**Theme H — 30% recycled-water debt service as the most defensible tranche (2 of 7).**
Two panelists (one at 70, one at 78) identified the debt-service allocation as squarely traceable to the cost rationale and likely to survive, supporting a "substantial part" rather than "whole" outcome.

**Theme I — Article X, §2 / Water Code §375 cannot override Prop 218's specific text (2 of 7, both at 70).**
Two panelists expressly reasoned that the conservation-mandate defense fails against the later-adopted, more specific §6(b) requirements.

**Cross-cutting uncertainty (flagged by all 7 in their uncertainty statements):** the unquantified gap between $0.85/hcf and what the 1.6x differential would strictly justify, which panelists uniformly identified as potentially outcome-determinative.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **Identity and holding of *Coziahr v. Otay Water District*.** Panelists cited three incompatible citations: (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 850, (2023) 91 Cal.App.5th 1, and (2024) 103 Cal.App.5th 122. One panelist relied on it affirmatively as deference-to-methodology authority; three others flagged its holding as unverified, and one was uncertain whether it narrows or reinforces *Capistrano*. The panel does not agree on what this case is or says.

2. **How strict *Capistrano* is versus the deference line of cases.** Some panelists treat the "in part incentive-pricing" admission as effectively fatal to the increment above cost (approaching a per se defect); others posit that *Wilde*/*Griffith*-style latitude for non-precise cost allocation could sustain the full $0.85 as a reasonable cost-based approximation. One panelist explicitly framed this as an unresolved doctrinal tension with no controlling Supreme Court decision.

3. **Which *Griffith* case supplies the countervailing authority.** One panelist relied on *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130 as tolerating conservation-oriented tiered pricing; four others cited *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586. These are different cases with different holdings being used for a similar purpose.

4. **What *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* stands for.** One panelist cited it for the agency's burden of proof by a preponderance; another cited it for rate-setting deference and the absence of a mathematical-precision requirement. These are different readings of the same authority.

5. **Whether the rebate program is defensible at all.** Most panelists treat the cross-subsidy concession as essentially indefensible under §6(b)(2)–(3). One panelist (78) identified a specific path by which the district could prevail on it — that rebates reduce system-wide peaking/drought-reserve costs and thereby benefit payors — while concluding the concessions make that a "harder sell."

6. **Whether any partial invalidation necessarily satisfies "in substantial part."** Most panelists assume partial invalidation satisfies the proposition as framed. One panelist (70) expressly disagreed with that assumption, reasoning that a "minor trim" to the rate might arguably not be "substantial," making the unquantified dollar magnitude the crux of whether the proposition is true.

7. **What *Bighorn* is authority for.** Most panelists cited *Bighorn* solely for the property-related-fee threshold; one panelist also cited it (with *Richmond v. Shasta*) as informing the substantive cross-subsidization/proportionality analysis.

## 4. Authorities

*Note: one panelist (at 70) submitted no formal authorities list, though that panelist's rationales expressly referenced Capistrano, Bighorn-Desert, and art. X, §2. Counts below reflect formal authorities lists; parentheticals note that panelist's in-rationale references where applicable.*

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(1)–(5) — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano* (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil* (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference; one panelist spelled "Vejil")
- Cal. Const. art. XIII C / §1(e) (Prop 26) — 6
- Cal. Const. art. X, §2 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- Water Code §375 — 6
- *Coziahr v. Otay Water District* — 5 (with divergent years and citations; see Disagreement #1; four of five flagged "verify")
- *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 — 4 (two flagged verification of pinpoint applicability)
- *Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District* (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 — 3
- *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — 2

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130
- *City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist.* (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926
- *Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist.* (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409
- *Zolly v. City of Oakland* (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 (cited with a "verify relevance" flag)
- Gov. Code §66022

---
*Round 2 instructions: Review the themes, disagreements, and authorities above. You may revise or maintain your probability and rationales in light of what other panelists raised.*

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":73,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"I move modestly upward from my round-1 estimate (70→73). The panel's consensus themes (A, B, C, E) essentially replicate my own round-1 reasoning and don't independently justify movement, but two points I under-weighted last round do: (1) the §6(b)(5) burden-of-proof allocation, combined with the district's own record concessions, makes the district's litigation posture worse than a neutral cost-allocation dispute — it must affirmatively prove compliance against its own adverse admissions; and (2) the 15% \"water ambassador\" staffing tranche is a third, independently vulnerable allocation (general public-education benefit rather than service to fee-payers) that I hadn't separately counted in round 1, further increasing the odds that some substantial component of the surcharge fails. Against this, the panel's Theme F/Disagreement #2 deference tension (Wilde/Griffith-line \"no mathematical precision required\" cases) is a genuine doctrinal counterweight I already partly captured via severability reasoning, and the unresolved/inconsistent Coziahr citations across the panel confirm that case should get ~zero evidentiary weight in either direction. Net effect: a modest upward revision, not a large one, because the core uncertainty — the unquantified gap between $0.85 and the 1.6x-justified figure, and whether a court reads \"derived in part from conservation-incentive pricing\" as fatal or as tolerable methodology commentary — remains genuinely unresolved on this record, and the proposition's \"in whole or in substantial part\" framing is broad enough that multiple independent, plausible partial-invalidation paths (pricing increment, rebate tranche, education-staff tranche) each separately satisfy it.","rationales":["The district bears the burden of proving §6(b) compliance by a preponderance of the evidence (Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(5); Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — verify precise burden holding), and the record here contains the agency's own concessions (conservation-incentive pricing driving part of the $0.85; rebates mostly benefiting non-payers) rather than a mere evidentiary gap the district could plausibly close at trial — this converts an ordinary methodology dispute into one where the plaintiff can point to admissions against interest on both defect theories simultaneously, which is unusually favorable for plaintiffs.","The 15% allocation to 'water ambassador' conservation-education staff is a third, analytically distinct vulnerability I did not separately weight in round 1: general conservation-education outreach looks like a general public benefit rather than a service to the specific parcels paying the surcharge, implicating §6(b)(2)'s prohibition on fee revenue exceeding costs attributable to the fee payer's parcel and the general-benefit-exclusion principle recognized post-Prop 218 (see Silicon Valley Taxpayers Assn. framework and Prop 26's general-benefit carve-out analog, art. XIII C §1(e)(2) — verify direct §6(b) applicability to staffing/education costs specifically). Combined with the rebate tranche (55%), a majority of the restricted fund's uses are colorably non-cost-correlated to payers, which cuts against the district's ability to characterize any defect as marginal.","The proposition's disjunctive framing ('in whole or in substantial part') is satisfied by any of several independently plausible partial-invalidation outcomes — striking the incentive-pricing increment above 1.6x, striking the 55% rebate tranche, or striking the 15% education tranche — so the panel's Disagreement #6 concern (that a 'minor trim' might not count as 'substantial') is a live risk only if a court finds none of these three defects material, which seems unlikely given three separate concession-backed theories on this record.","The countervailing deference line (Wilde/Griffith 'no mathematical precision required') is real and prevents me from placing this much higher than 73: Capistrano's holding does not require exact marginal-cost equivalence, and a court sympathetic to conservation policy could characterize the rate study's 'in part' language as reasonable, non-fatal commentary on a rate that is still substantially anchored to the 1.6x cost differential, particularly if the unstated dollar gap between $0.85 and a strict 1.6x-derived figure turns out to be small — this remains the single largest source of downside risk to the proposition and is why I don't move above the mid-70s.","The panel's inability to agree on Coziahr v. Otay Water District's citation, year, or holding (three incompatible citations offered) means it should not move my estimate in either direction; I am also declining to lean on Zolly v. City of Oakland (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 given one panelist's unresolved 'verify relevance' flag, and I continue to treat Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 as the more likely-relevant Griffith case over Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz, but flag both for verification given the panel split."],"authorities":["Cal. Const. art. XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(5)","Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205","Cal. Const. art. X, § 2","Water Code § 375","Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop 26 'tax' definition and fee/general-benefit exceptions)","Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — verify precise holding on burden of proof vs. rate-setting deference (panel split on what it stands for)","Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 — verify pinpoint applicability; distinguish from Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130","Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372","Coziahr v. Otay Water District — citation unresolved across panel (2018/2023/2024 variants offered); verify identity and holding before relying on it at all","Zolly v. City of Oakland (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 — relevance unverified"],"biggest_uncertainty":"The unquantified dollar gap between the $0.85/hcf surcharge and what the documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential would strictly justify remains the single largest driver of outcome variance — if that gap is small, a court may treat the \"conservation-incentive pricing\" language as harmless rhetorical framing around an essentially cost-justified rate (favoring the district under the Wilde/Griffith deference line); if the gap is large, it looks like a near-admission of the exact defect Capistrano condemned. Secondary to this is genuine, currently irresolvable uncertainty about how strictly the post-Capistrano appellate landscape (particularly whatever Coziahr actually holds, which the panel could not agree on) has either hardened or softened the cost-correlation standard for tiered/conservation pricing since 2015.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"Two panel considerations genuinely moved me upward from 70 to 73: Theme D (§6(b)(5) burden of proof falling on the district, compounded by its own concessions) and Theme G (the 15% education-staffing tranche as a third independent general-benefit vulnerability), neither of which I had weighted in round 1. Both survive my own independent scrutiny as legally sound rather than merely popular. I did not move further toward the higher-probability panelists (72, 78) because Theme F's deference counterweight and Disagreement #2's doctrinal tension are, in my independent judgment, real and unresolved, not adequately discounted by the majority's confidence — Capistrano's condemnation of pure conservation-pricing does not obviously extend to a rate that is still majority-anchored to a documented 1.6x cost study, and I won't assume the strict reading prevails just because more panelists lean that way. I also declined to let Theme F's citation to Coziahr push me toward the 72/78 cluster, since the panel itself could not agree what that case says — citation instability across independent panelists is itself evidence the authority is being invoked more as a placeholder for 'deference exists somewhere' than as a verified, load-bearing holding, so I gave it zero net weight rather than treating panel volume as validation."}

data.position_summary: I move modestly upward from my round-1 estimate (70→73). The panel's consensus themes (A, B, C, E) essentially replicate my own round-1 reasoning and don't independently justify movement, but two points I under-weighted last round do: (1) the §6(b)(5) burden-of-proof allocation, combined with the district's own record concessions, makes the district's litigation posture worse than a neutral cost-allocation dispute — it must affirmatively prove compliance against its own adverse admissions; and (2) the 15% "water ambassador" staffing tranche is a third, independently vulnerable allocation (general public-education benefit rather than service to fee-payers) that I hadn't separately counted in round 1, further increasing the odds that some substantial component of the surcharge fails. Against this, the panel's Theme F/Disagreement #2 deference tension (Wilde/Griffith-line "no mathematical precision required" cases) is a genuine doctrinal counterweight I already partly captured via severability reasoning, and the unresolved/inconsistent Coziahr citations across the panel confirm that case should get ~zero evidentiary weight in either direction. Net effect: a modest upward revision, not a large one, because the core uncertainty — the unquantified gap between $0.85 and the 1.6x-justified figure, and whether a court reads "derived in part from conservation-incentive pricing" as fatal or as tolerable methodology commentary — remains genuinely unresolved on this record, and the proposition's "in whole or in substantial part" framing is broad enough that multiple independent, plausible partial-invalidation paths (pricing increment, rebate tranche, education-staff tranche) each separately satisfy it.
rationales: The district bears the burden of proving §6(b) compliance by a preponderance of the evidence (Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(5); Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — verify precise burden holding), and the record here contains the agency's own concessions (conservation-incentive pricing driving part of the $0.85; rebates mostly benefiting non-payers) rather than a mere evidentiary gap the district could plausibly close at trial — this converts an ordinary methodology dispute into one where the plaintiff can point to admissions against interest on both defect theories simultaneously, which is unusually favorable for plaintiffs.

The 15% allocation to 'water ambassador' conservation-education staff is a third, analytically distinct vulnerability I did not separately weight in round 1: general conservation-education outreach looks like a general public benefit rather than a service to the specific parcels paying the surcharge, implicating §6(b)(2)'s prohibition on fee revenue exceeding costs attributable to the fee payer's parcel and the general-benefit-exclusion principle recognized post-Prop 218 (see Silicon Valley Taxpayers Assn. framework and Prop 26's general-benefit carve-out analog, art. XIII C §1(e)(2) — verify direct §6(b) applicability to staffing/education costs specifically). Combined with the rebate tranche (55%), a majority of the restricted fund's uses are colorably non-cost-correlated to payers, which cuts against the district's ability to characterize any defect as marginal.

The proposition's disjunctive framing ('in whole or in substantial part') is satisfied by any of several independently plausible partial-invalidation outcomes — striking the incentive-pricing increment above 1.6x, striking the 55% rebate tranche, or striking the 15% education tranche — so the panel's Disagreement #6 concern (that a 'minor trim' might not count as 'substantial') is a live risk only if a court finds none of these three defects material, which seems unlikely given three separate concession-backed theories on this record.

The countervailing deference line (Wilde/Griffith 'no mathematical precision required') is real and prevents me from placing this much higher than 73: Capistrano's holding does not require exact marginal-cost equivalence, and a court sympathetic to conservation policy could characterize the rate study's 'in part' language as reasonable, non-fatal commentary on a rate that is still substantially anchored to the 1.6x cost differential, particularly if the unstated dollar gap between $0.85 and a strict 1.6x-derived figure turns out to be small — this remains the single largest source of downside risk to the proposition and is why I don't move above the mid-70s.

The panel's inability to agree on Coziahr v. Otay Water District's citation, year, or holding (three incompatible citations offered) means it should not move my estimate in either direction; I am also declining to lean on Zolly v. City of Oakland (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 given one panelist's unresolved 'verify relevance' flag, and I continue to treat Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 as the more likely-relevant Griffith case over Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz, but flag both for verification given the panel split.
authorities: Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493
Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205
Cal. Const. art. XIII C, § 1(e) (Prop 26 'tax' definition and fee/general-benefit exceptions)
Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — verify precise holding on burden of proof vs. rate-setting deference (panel split on what it stands for)
Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 — verify pinpoint applicability; distinguish from Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130
Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372
Coziahr v. Otay Water District — citation unresolved across panel (2018/2023/2024 variants offered); verify identity and holding before relying on it at all
Zolly v. City of Oakland (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 — relevance unverified
data.biggest_uncertainty: The unquantified dollar gap between the $0.85/hcf surcharge and what the documented 1.6x marginal-cost differential would strictly justify remains the single largest driver of outcome variance — if that gap is small, a court may treat the "conservation-incentive pricing" language as harmless rhetorical framing around an essentially cost-justified rate (favoring the district under the Wilde/Griffith deference line); if the gap is large, it looks like a near-admission of the exact defect Capistrano condemned. Secondary to this is genuine, currently irresolvable uncertainty about how strictly the post-Capistrano appellate landscape (particularly whatever Coziahr actually holds, which the panel could not agree on) has either hardened or softened the cost-correlation standard for tiered/conservation pricing since 2015.
data.response_to_panel: Two panel considerations genuinely moved me upward from 70 to 73: Theme D (§6(b)(5) burden of proof falling on the district, compounded by its own concessions) and Theme G (the 15% education-staffing tranche as a third independent general-benefit vulnerability), neither of which I had weighted in round 1. Both survive my own independent scrutiny as legally sound rather than merely popular. I did not move further toward the higher-probability panelists (72, 78) because Theme F's deference counterweight and Disagreement #2's doctrinal tension are, in my independent judgment, real and unresolved, not adequately discounted by the majority's confidence — Capistrano's condemnation of pure conservation-pricing does not obviously extend to a rate that is still majority-anchored to a documented 1.6x cost study, and I won't assume the strict reading prevails just because more panelists lean that way. I also declined to let Theme F's citation to Coziahr push me toward the 72/78 cluster, since the panel itself could not agree what that case says — citation instability across independent panelists is itself evidence the authority is being invoked more as a placeholder for 'deference exists somewhere' than as a verified, load-bearing holding, so I gave it zero net weight rather than treating panel volume as validation.
